does: This paper introduces AgentLens, a white-box defense framework that detects and mitigates unsafe behavior in multi-turn coding agents through mechanistic interpretability. The method operates by analyzing step-level hidden representations from an LLM-based agent to identify harmful execution states and performing representation-level interventions in low-dimensional mechanistic subspaces. Rather than relying on external guardrails applied at the model input or output level, AgentLens works at the internal activation level during runtime, enabling fine-grained behavioral control across multiple interaction turns. The framework appears to achieve safety steering by identifying and manipulating a discovered 10-dimensional safety-relevant subspace within the model's hidden representations, allowing intervention in the agent's reasoning process as it unfolds across multi-step trajectories.
connects: This work builds on two converging research directions in LLM safety. First, it extends recent mechanistic interpretability methods that probe internal model representations to understand and control model behavior, building on techniques developed for single-turn adversarial robustness and jailbreak detection. Second, it addresses limitations in existing agent safety frameworks, which have primarily relied on external guardrails and prompt-based constraints that lack visibility into the agent's evolving internal state during multi-turn execution. The paper directly responds to the gap between single-turn safety mechanisms and the more complex safety challenges posed by autonomous agents interacting with external environments over multiple steps. It combines insights from interpretability-based safety interventions with agent-specific threat modeling.
enables: This work opens several future directions for interpretable AI safety in autonomous systems. It demonstrates that safety-relevant information can be extracted and steered at the mechanistic level in sequential decision-making settings, potentially enabling safer deployment of coding agents and other autonomous LLM-based systems. The approach of intervening in low-dimensional safety subspaces provides a template for building transparent, interpretable safety mechanisms that operate at runtime rather than requiring retraining or external oversight. Future work could extend this framework to other agent modalities, explore how safety subspaces generalize across different agent architectures or tasks, develop better methods for automatically discovering safety-relevant dimensions, and investigate whether mechanistic steering can provide formal safety guarantees for long-horizon agent behavior.
